diagnosis: Atrial flutter. Don't miss hidden atrial (F) wave just after QRS. This is atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction. Note that with atrial flutter there aren't P waves per se but atrial waves. So if you talk about atrial flutter do not say "the P waves...." since once you say P wave, the informed listener will probably assume it's not atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter. On the other hand, depending on whom you are talking to, this may not be an issue. Also note left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) and left axis with possible prior inferior wall myocardial infarction (MI) here.  Note that typical flutter waves have negative polarity in lead II, as is the case here.

report.rate: Ventricular rate: ~150 bpm; Atrial rate: ~300 bpm
report.intervals: PR: N/A (Atrial flutter); QRS: ~90 ms; QTc: ~470 ms
report.findings: Typical sawtooth atrial flutter waves (negative polarity in leads II, III, aVF); 2:1 AV conduction with hidden F-waves following the QRS complexes; Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) by voltage (SV2 + RV5 > 35mm) with associated ST-T wave abnormalities (strain pattern) in lateral leads; Possible prior inferior wall myocardial infarction based on Q-waves/rS complexes in inferior leads.
report.impression: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction (ventricular rate 150 bpm), left axis deviation, and left ventricular hypertrophy with secondary repolarization changes.
